raw_query: Consumer behavior patterns in online and digital markets.

refined_query.search_strategy: This refined search focuses on academic literature concerning consumer behavior within digital markets, utilizing key concepts like behavioral economics and consumer psychology, and excluding social media marketing to narrow the scope to traditional online and e-commerce contexts.

papers.title: How marketing vocabulary was evolving from 2005 to 2014? An illustrative application of statistical methods on text mining
papers.abstract: Here a collection of 1169 abstracts, which corresponds to articles that the Journal of Marketing Research has published from 2005 to 2014, are analysed under a novel approach. We apply several statistical methods, such as Principal Components Analysis and Correspondence Analysis to identify the way Marketing vocabulary is evolving. Similarly those articles that introduce new vocabulary are identified and the preferred words by authors are also detected. In order to provide an easy-to-understand explanation, we provide our results graphically. A word-cloud with the most frequent words is given first. Secondly abstracts-words are represented on the factorial plane. Finally one representation of word-years allows us to detect changes on the vocabulary through the passing of time.

papers.title: A Novel Approach to Analyze Fashion Digital Archive from Humanities
papers.abstract: Fashion styles adopted every day are an important aspect of culture, and style trend analysis helps provide a deeper understanding of our societies and cultures. To analyze everyday fashion trends from the humanities perspective, we need a digital archive that includes images of what people wore in their daily lives over an extended period. In fashion research, building digital fashion image archives has attracted significant attention. However, the existing archives are not suitable for retrieving everyday fashion trends. In addition, to interpret how the trends emerge, we need non-fashion data sources relevant to why and how people choose fashion. In this study, we created a new fashion image archive called Chronicle Archive of Tokyo Street Fashion (CAT STREET) based on a review of the limitations in the existing digital fashion archives. CAT STREET includes images showing the clothing people wore in their daily lives during the period 1970--2017, which contain timestamps and street location annotations. We applied machine learning to CAT STREET and found two types of fashion trend patterns. Then, we demonstrated how magazine archives help us interpret how trend patterns emerge. These empirical analyses show our approach's potential to discover new perspectives to promote an understanding of our societies and cultures through fashion embedded in consumers' daily lives.

papers.title: Semantic Equivalence of e-Commerce Queries
papers.abstract: Search query variation poses a challenge in e-commerce search, as equivalent search intents can be expressed through different queries with surface-level differences. This paper introduces a framework to recognize and leverage query equivalence to enhance searcher and business outcomes. The proposed approach addresses three key problems: mapping queries to vector representations of search intent, identifying nearest neighbor queries expressing equivalent or similar intent, and optimizing for user or business objectives. The framework utilizes both surface similarity and behavioral similarity to determine query equivalence. Surface similarity involves canonicalizing queries based on word inflection, word order, compounding, and noise words. Behavioral similarity leverages historical search behavior to generate vector representations of query intent. An offline process is used to train a sentence similarity model, while an online nearest neighbor approach supports processing of unseen queries. Experimental evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, outperforming popular sentence transformer models and achieving a Pearson correlation of 0.85 for query similarity. The results highlight the potential of leveraging historical behavior data and training models to recognize and utilize query equivalence in e-commerce search, leading to improved user experiences and business outcomes. Further advancements and benchmark datasets are encouraged to facilitate the development of solutions for this critical problem in the e-commerce domain.

papers.title: Online consumer resilience during a pandemic: An exploratory study of e-commerce behavior before, during and after a COVID-19 lockdown
papers.abstract: The COVID-19 pandemic has disrupted retail and accelerated the trend towards electronic commerce. This study explores the reasons for and the implications of this shift. Our study builds on the consumer behavior literature, emerging COVID-19 research, and the environmentally imposed constraints perspective to describe how online purchasing behavior evolved during the COVID-19 crisis. The objective is to better understand how consumers use e-commerce to react to, cope with and adapt to periods of environmentally imposed constraints. Based on multiple sources including transaction and search data from a major French online retailer, we describe how consumer behavior evolves during such stressful life events as COVID-19. Our results support the usefulness of the multi-perspective react-cope-adapt framework of constrained consumer behavior in an online environment.

papers.title: Understanding Online Consumer Behavior at E-commerce Portals Using Eye-Gaze Tracking
papers.abstract: Abstract E-commerce websites are doing extremely well despite the global slowdown in the traditional brick-and-mortar retail industry. Web page design, search options, and graphical layout may greatly influence the customer base of any e-commerce website. This paper intends to identify the most influential elements of a graphical interface and their effect on consumers’ visual attention. Visual layouts having major influence on customers’ decisions during online shopping were investigated using eye-gaze tracking. Data from 76 respondents were collected to identify parts of the graphical interface attaining maximum user attention in terms of first fixation of eye-gaze and fixation count. Heat maps were used to visualize the results. The results revealed a clear impact of graphical interface on gaining user attention. Further, the experiment also revealed gender-specific visual preferences toward various parts of graphical user interface. The results may further be extended to evaluate product design, packaging design, promotional design, and graphical interfaces of websites.

2107.08351v2.title: A Novel Approach to Analyze Fashion Digital Archive from Humanities

sections.abstract: This study created the Chronicle Archive of Tokyo Street Fashion (CAT STREET), a digital archive of daily fashion images from 1970-2017, to analyze fashion trends. Machine learning was applied to identify two types of fashion trend patterns, and magazine archives were used to interpret their emergence, demonstrating a potential approach to understanding consumer behavior in digital markets through fashion.
sections.introduction: The research addresses the limitations of existing digital fashion archives, which are not suitable for retrieving everyday fashion trends. It highlights the need for non-fashion data sources to understand consumer choices and the creation of a new archive (CAT STREET) with timestamps and location annotations to analyze fashion trends.
sections.methodology: The methodology involved creating the CAT STREET archive, a digital fashion image archive containing images of daily fashion from 1970-2017. Machine learning was applied to the archive to identify two types of fashion trend patterns.  The research also utilized magazine archives to interpret the emergence of these trends.
sections.results: Machine learning analysis of the CAT STREET archive revealed two distinct types of fashion trend patterns. The study demonstrated the utility of magazine archives in interpreting the origins and development of these trends.
sections.discussion: The research suggests that analyzing fashion trends embedded in consumers' daily lives, as captured in a digital archive, offers a new perspective on understanding societies and cultures. The findings highlight the potential of digital archives and machine learning to uncover consumer behavior patterns in online and digital markets, specifically within the fashion sector.
sections.related_work: The study acknowledges existing digital fashion archives but identifies their limitations. It positions itself as a novel approach to analyzing fashion trends by incorporating contextual data (timestamps, location) and employing machine learning techniques, building upon previous research in digital fashion archiving.
sections.conclusion: The creation of CAT STREET and the application of machine learning to identify and interpret fashion trends demonstrate a viable approach to analyzing consumer behavior in digital markets, particularly within the fashion industry. The research’s findings contribute to a deeper understanding of how trends emerge and are adopted by consumers.
key_quotes: ‘To analyze everyday fashion trends from the humanities perspective, we need a digital archive that includes images of what people wore in their daily lives over an extended period.’
‘These empirical analyses show our approach’s potential to discover new perspectives to promote an understanding of our societies and cultures through fashion embedded in consumers’ daily lives.’

key_findings_summary.finding: Two types of fashion trend patterns were identified within the CAT STREET archive using machine learning.
key_findings_summary.evidence: "We applied machine learning to CAT STREET and found two types of fashion trend patterns."

The approach achieves a Pearson correlation of 0.85, demonstrating the potential for improved user experiences and business outcomes.
sections.introduction: The paper addresses the challenge of search query variation in e-commerce, highlighting that equivalent search intents can be expressed through different queries with surface-level differences. It introduces a framework to tackle this problem by mapping queries to vector representations and identifying similar intent.
sections.methodology: The framework employs a two-pronged approach: 1) Surface similarity analysis through canonicalization of queries (word inflection, order, compounding, noise words) and 2) Behavioral similarity leveraging historical search data to generate vector representations of query intent. An offline sentence similarity model is trained, and an online nearest neighbor approach is used for processing unseen queries.
sections.results: Experimental evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, outperforming popular sentence transformer models with a Pearson correlation of 0.85 for query similarity. This indicates a strong ability to identify equivalent or similar search intents.
sections.discussion: The results highlight the potential of leveraging historical behavior data and training models to recognize and utilize query equivalence in e-commerce search, leading to improved user experiences and business outcomes. The framework's performance suggests a viable solution for addressing the challenge of query variation in e-commerce search.
sections.related_work: The paper implicitly relates to research on semantic search and information retrieval, specifically focusing on sentence similarity models and vector representations of search intent. It contrasts with approaches that solely rely on surface-level matching, incorporating behavioral data for a more nuanced understanding of user intent.
sections.conclusion: The research demonstrates the effectiveness of a framework utilizing both surface and behavioral similarity to identify query equivalence in e-commerce search, offering a promising approach for enhancing search relevance and improving user and business outcomes. Further development and benchmark datasets are encouraged.

10.1016/j.jretconser.2021.102570.title: Online consumer resilience during a pandemic: An exploratory study of e-commerce behavior before, during and after a COVID-19 lockdown

sections.abstract: The COVID-19 pandemic has disrupted retail and accelerated the trend towards electronic commerce. This study explores the reasons for and the implications of this shift, focusing on how consumer behavior evolved during the crisis and the usefulness of a constrained consumer behavior framework in an online environment.
sections.introduction: The study’s objective is to understand how consumers use e-commerce to react to, cope with, and adapt to periods of environmentally imposed constraints (like COVID-19 lockdowns) as evidenced by a shift in purchasing behavior from traditional retail to online channels.
sections.methodology: The study utilizes transaction and search data from a major French online retailer to describe consumer behavior evolution during the COVID-19 crisis. It leverages a multi-perspective react-cope-adapt framework.
sections.results: The results support the usefulness of the multi-perspective react-cope-adapt framework of constrained consumer behavior in an online environment, demonstrating how consumer behavior evolved during the stressful life event of the pandemic.
sections.discussion: The findings highlight how consumers adapted their purchasing patterns in response to the imposed constraints of the lockdown, indicating a shift in consumer behavior towards e-commerce.
sections.related_work: The research builds on consumer behavior literature, emerging COVID-19 research, and the environmentally imposed constraints perspective, positioning itself within the broader field of understanding consumer responses to disruptive events.
sections.conclusion: The study concludes that understanding consumer resilience during periods of environmental constraint, as exemplified by the COVID-19 pandemic, is crucial for understanding and predicting e-commerce behavior.
key_quotes: ‘Our results support the usefulness of the multi-perspective react-cope-adapt framework of constrained consumer behavior in an online environment.’
‘This study explores the reasons for and the implications of this shift.’

key_findings_summary.finding: Consumer behavior evolved during the COVID-19 crisis, as evidenced by the analysis of e-commerce data.

10.1080/10447318.2022.2047318.title: Understanding Online Consumer Behavior at E-commerce Portals Using Eye-Gaze Tracking

sections.abstract: E-commerce websites are thriving despite economic headwinds. This paper investigates the impact of graphical interface elements on consumer visual attention, utilizing eye-gaze tracking on 76 respondents to identify areas of maximum user attention and gender-specific visual preferences. The research aims to understand how web page design influences customer decisions during online shopping.
sections.introduction: The paper establishes the context of successful e-commerce despite broader retail challenges. It highlights the importance of web page design, search options, and graphical layout in influencing customer behavior and specifically focuses on understanding how visual attention is directed by these elements within an e-commerce portal.
sections.methodology: The study employed eye-gaze tracking with 76 respondents to analyze user attention on an e-commerce website. Data was collected on first fixation of eye-gaze and fixation count, visualized using heat maps to identify areas of highest user attention.  The research also explored gender-specific visual preferences.
sections.results: The experiment revealed a significant impact of the graphical interface on gaining user attention.  Specifically, the study identified areas of the interface that attracted the most user attention and demonstrated gender-specific visual preferences toward different parts of the graphical user interface.
sections.discussion: The findings suggest that visual layout plays a crucial role in directing consumer attention during online shopping. The identification of gender-specific preferences indicates the need for tailored design approaches to optimize engagement and conversion rates. The results have implications for product, packaging, and promotional design, as well as website graphical interfaces.
sections.related_work: The research builds upon existing work examining the influence of web design on consumer behavior, extending previous studies by utilizing eye-gaze tracking to provide a more granular understanding of visual attention patterns and incorporating gender-specific analysis.
sections.conclusion: The study concludes that graphical interface design significantly impacts consumer attention and engagement in online shopping, with notable gender-specific variations. The findings provide valuable insights for optimizing e-commerce website design to improve user experience and drive sales.
key_quotes: ‘A clear impact of graphical interface on gaining user attention’
‘gender-specific visual preferences toward various parts of graphical user interface’

key_findings_summary.finding: Graphical interface elements significantly impact user attention, as evidenced by first fixation of eye-gaze and fixation count.
key_findings_summary.evidence: "The results revealed a clear impact of graphical interface on gaining user attention."

key_findings_summary.finding: There are gender-specific visual preferences toward different parts of the graphical user interface.
key_findings_summary.evidence: "Further, the experiment also revealed gender-specific visual preferences toward various parts of graphical user interface."

feature_relationships.feature1: Query Equivalence & Semantic Similarity in E-commerce Search
feature_relationships.feature2: Trend Pattern Identification in Fashion Digital Archives
feature_relationships.description: Both papers explore the use of data analysis (query similarity vs. trend identification) to understand consumer behavior, albeit in different domains. The e-commerce paper builds on the concept of finding similar intent, while the fashion paper uses machine learning to identify patterns within a digital archive.

feature_relationships.feature2: Evolution of Consumer Behavior During Disruptive Events
feature_relationships.description: The first paper provides a broader framework for understanding how consumers react to crises, while the second paper applies that framework specifically to the COVID-19 pandemic and e-commerce behavior.

The second paper directly investigates how graphical interface elements influence user attention, building upon the broader understanding of visual perception and user experience gained from eye-gaze tracking research.

While one uses statistical methods to analyze language and the other uses machine learning for trend discovery, both contribute to understanding how consumers interpret and respond to marketing information.

cluster_analysis.description: Cluster 1 focuses on foundational analysis of marketing language and trend identification. Cluster 2 explores more applied research using different methodologies.

relationships.research_progression: The research landscape in this area has evolved from primarily linguistic analysis of marketing vocabulary (Paper 1) to incorporating more sophisticated data analysis techniques, including machine learning and eye-tracking. Initially, the focus was on understanding the *language* of marketing, then shifted to identifying *patterns* in consumer behavior across various digital channels, and finally to understanding the *visual* cues that influence consumer attention. The pandemic significantly accelerated the adoption of e-commerce and prompted research into consumer resilience and adaptation.
key_connections: The increasing use of machine learning and data analytics is a key trend in understanding consumer behavior in online and digital markets.
The integration of different data sources (e.g., search queries, eye-gaze data, digital archives) is becoming increasingly important for gaining a holistic view of consumer behavior.
Understanding consumer resilience during disruptive events is a critical area of research for navigating future crises.

properties.title: How marketing vocabulary was evolving from 2005 to 2014? An illustrative application of statistical methods on text mining
properties.abstract: Here a collection of 1169 abstracts, which corresponds to articles that the Journal of Marketing Research has published from 2005 to 2014, are analysed under a novel approach. We apply several statistical methods, such as Principal Components Analysis and Correspondence Analysis to identify the way Marketing vocabulary is evolving. Similarly those articles that introduce new vocabulary are identified and the preferred words by authors are also detected. In order to provide an easy-to-understand

key_findings.finding: The study identifies the most frequent words used in marketing abstracts from 2005-2014.
key_findings.evidence: "A word-cloud with the most frequent words is given first."

key_findings.finding: Abstracts were represented on a factorial plane to visualize word relationships.
key_findings.evidence: "Secondly abstracts-words are represented on the factorial plane."

key_findings.finding: Changes in marketing vocabulary were detected over time using a word-years representation.
key_findings.evidence: "Finally one representation of word-years allows us to detect changes on the vocabulary through the passing of time."

methodology.approach: The study employs statistical methods, including Principal Components Analysis and Correspondence Analysis, alongside text mining techniques to analyze 1169 abstracts published in the Journal of Marketing Research between 2005 and 2014. Graphical representations, such as word clouds, factorial planes, and word-years representations, are used to visualize the findings.
methodology.evidence: "We apply several statistical methods, such as Principal Components Analysis and Correspondence Analysis to identify the way Marketing vocabulary is evolving."

limitations: The analysis is limited to abstracts published in the Journal of Marketing Research, potentially excluding other relevant marketing literature.
The specific statistical methods employed may not capture all nuances of vocabulary evolution.

sections.abstract: This research analyzes 1169 abstracts from the Journal of Marketing Research (2005-2014) using statistical methods (Principal Components Analysis, Correspondence Analysis) to identify evolving marketing vocabulary and preferred author words. The analysis employs graphical representations including word clouds, factorial planes, and word-years to illustrate these changes.
sections.introduction: The paper’s introduction establishes the context of evolving marketing vocabulary and the need to understand trends in academic research. It sets the stage for a quantitative investigation into the evolution of marketing terminology within a specific journal’s publication record.
sections.methodology: The core methodology involves applying statistical techniques – Principal Components Analysis and Correspondence Analysis – to a dataset of 1169 abstracts. The research also identifies new vocabulary introduced and analyzes word frequency trends over time, utilizing graphical representations for clarity.
sections.results: The research identifies the most frequent words within the abstracts, creating a word-cloud.  It represents abstract words on a factorial plane and generates a representation of word-years to detect vocabulary changes over the 2005-2014 period. The analysis reveals patterns in the evolution of marketing vocabulary.
sections.discussion: The discussion interprets the results of the statistical analyses, highlighting the identified trends in marketing vocabulary. The graphical representations (word clouds, factorial planes, word-years) are presented as tools for understanding these shifts.
sections.related_work: The paper implicitly relates to broader research on marketing terminology and the use of text mining techniques in marketing research. It positions itself as a novel application of statistical methods to a specific corpus of marketing literature.
sections.conclusion: The paper concludes that marketing vocabulary evolved between 2005 and 2014, as evidenced by the statistical analysis and graphical representations. The study provides a visual and quantitative understanding of these changes.
key_quotes: ‘We apply several statistical methods…to identify the way Marketing vocabulary is evolving.’
‘…we provide our results graphically. A word-cloud with the most frequent words is given first.’

properties.title: A Novel Approach to Analyze Fashion Digital Archive from Humanities
properties.abstract: Fashion styles adopted every day are an important aspect of culture, and style trend analysis helps provide a deeper understanding of our societies and cultures. To analyze everyday fashion trends from the humanities perspective, we need a digital archive that includes images of what people wore in their daily lives over an extended period. In fashion research, building digital fashion image archives has attracted significant attention. However, the existing archives are not suitable for retriev

key_findings.finding: Two types of fashion trend patterns were identified within the CAT STREET archive using machine learning.
key_findings.evidence: "We applied machine learning to CAT STREET and found two types of fashion trend patterns."

methodology.approach: The study created a new digital fashion archive (CAT STREET) and applied machine learning techniques to analyze the image data, supplemented by analysis of magazine archives.
methodology.evidence: "We created a new fashion image archive called Chronicle Archive of Tokyo Street Fashion (CAT STREET) based on a review of the limitations in the existing digital fashion archives."

limitations: The study focuses solely on Tokyo street fashion, limiting the generalizability of the findings.
The specific machine learning techniques used are not detailed, potentially impacting reproducibility.

sections.abstract: This study created the Chronicle Archive of Tokyo Street Fashion (CAT STREET), a digital archive of daily fashion images from 1970-2017, to analyze fashion trends. Machine learning was applied to identify two types of fashion trend patterns, and magazine archives were used to interpret their emergence, demonstrating a potential approach to understanding consumer behavior in digital markets through fashion.
sections.introduction: The research addresses the limitations of existing digital fashion archives, which are not suitable for retrieving everyday fashion trends. It highlights the need for non-fashion data sources to understand consumer choices and the creation of a new archive (CAT STREET) with timestamps and location annotations to analyze fashion trends.
sections.methodology: The methodology involved creating the CAT STREET archive, a digital fashion image archive containing images of daily fashion from 1970-2017. Machine learning was applied to the archive to identify two types of fashion trend patterns.  The research also utilized magazine archives to interpret the emergence of these trends.
sections.results: Machine learning analysis of the CAT STREET archive revealed two distinct types of fashion trend patterns. The study demonstrated the utility of magazine archives in interpreting the origins and development of these trends.
sections.discussion: The research suggests that analyzing fashion trends embedded in consumers' daily lives, as captured in a digital archive, offers a new perspective on understanding societies and cultures. The findings highlight the potential of digital archives and machine learning to uncover consumer behavior patterns in online and digital markets, specifically within the fashion sector.
sections.related_work: The study acknowledges existing digital fashion archives but identifies their limitations. It positions itself as a novel approach to analyzing fashion trends by incorporating contextual data (timestamps, location) and employing machine learning techniques, building upon previous research in digital fashion archiving.
sections.conclusion: The creation of CAT STREET and the application of machine learning to identify and interpret fashion trends demonstrate a viable approach to analyzing consumer behavior in digital markets, particularly within the fashion industry. The research’s findings contribute to a deeper understanding of how trends emerge and are adopted by consumers.
key_quotes: ‘To analyze everyday fashion trends from the humanities perspective, we need a digital archive that includes images of what people wore in their daily lives over an extended period.’
‘These empirical analyses show our approach’s potential to discover new perspectives to promote an understanding of our societies and cultures through fashion embedded in consumers’ daily lives.’

properties.title: Semantic Equivalence of e-Commerce Queries
properties.abstract: Search query variation poses a challenge in e-commerce search, as equivalent search intents can be expressed through different queries with surface-level differences. This paper introduces a framework to recognize and leverage query equivalence to enhance searcher and business outcomes. The proposed approach addresses three key problems: mapping queries to vector representations of search intent, identifying nearest neighbor queries expressing equivalent or similar intent, and optimizing for use

key_findings.finding: The framework outperforms popular sentence transformer models.
key_findings.evidence: "Experimental evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, outperforming popular sentence transformer models"

key_findings.finding: A Pearson correlation of 0.85 was achieved for query similarity.
key_findings.evidence: "...achieving a Pearson correlation of 0.85 for query similarity."

key_findings.finding: The approach leverages historical search behavior to generate query intent vectors.
key_findings.evidence: "Behavioral similarity leverages historical search behavior to generate vector representations of query intent."

methodology.approach: The paper introduces a framework utilizing both surface similarity and behavioral similarity to determine query equivalence. It employs an offline sentence similarity model training process and an online nearest neighbor approach.
methodology.evidence: "The proposed approach addresses three key problems: mapping queries to vector representations of search intent, identifying nearest neighbor queries expressing equivalent or similar intent, and optimizing for user or business objectives."

limitations: The paper does not specify the exact datasets used for training or evaluation.
The effectiveness of the approach may depend on the quality and availability of historical search behavior data.

sections.abstract: This paper introduces a framework to recognize and leverage query equivalence in e-commerce search, utilizing both surface similarity (canonicalization) and behavioral similarity (historical search data) to map queries to vector representations of search intent and identify nearest neighbor queries expressing equivalent or similar intent. The approach achieves a Pearson correlation of 0.85, demonstrating the potential for improved user experiences and business outcomes.
sections.introduction: The paper addresses the challenge of search query variation in e-commerce, highlighting that equivalent search intents can be expressed through different queries with surface-level differences. It introduces a framework to tackle this problem by mapping queries to vector representations and identifying similar intent.
sections.methodology: The framework employs a two-pronged approach: 1) Surface similarity analysis through canonicalization of queries (word inflection, order, compounding, noise words) and 2) Behavioral similarity leveraging historical search data to generate vector representations of query intent. An offline sentence similarity model is trained, and an online nearest neighbor approach is used for processing unseen queries.
sections.results: Experimental evaluations demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed approach, outperforming popular sentence transformer models with a Pearson correlation of 0.85 for query similarity. This indicates a strong ability to identify equivalent or similar search intents.
sections.discussion: The results highlight the potential of leveraging historical behavior data and training models to recognize and utilize query equivalence in e-commerce search, leading to improved user experiences and business outcomes. The framework's performance suggests a viable solution for addressing the challenge of query variation in e-commerce search.
sections.related_work: The paper implicitly relates to research on semantic search and information retrieval, specifically focusing on sentence similarity models and vector representations of search intent. It contrasts with approaches that solely rely on surface-level matching, incorporating behavioral data for a more nuanced understanding of user intent.
sections.conclusion: The research demonstrates the effectiveness of a framework utilizing both surface and behavioral similarity to identify query equivalence in e-commerce search, offering a promising approach for enhancing search relevance and improving user and business outcomes. Further development and benchmark datasets are encouraged.
key_quotes: The framework utilizes both surface similarity and behavioral similarity to determine query equivalence.
Achieving a Pearson correlation of 0.85 for query similarity.

properties.title: Online consumer resilience during a pandemic: An exploratory study of e-commerce behavior before, during and after a COVID-19 lockdown
properties.abstract: The COVID-19 pandemic has disrupted retail and accelerated the trend towards electronic commerce. This study explores the reasons for and the implications of this shift. Our study builds on the consumer behavior literature, emerging COVID-19 research, and the environmentally imposed constraints perspective to describe how online purchasing behavior evolved during the COVID-19 crisis. The objective is to better understand how consumers use e-commerce to react to, cope with and adapt to periods of

key_findings.finding: Consumer behavior evolved during the COVID-19 crisis, as evidenced by the analysis of e-commerce data.
key_findings.evidence: ‘We describe how consumer behavior evolves during such stressful life events as COVID-19.’

methodology.approach: Exploratory study utilizing transaction and search data from a major French online retailer to examine consumer behavior changes during the COVID-19 pandemic.
methodology.evidence: ‘Our results support the usefulness of the multi-perspective react-cope-adapt framework of constrained consumer behavior in an online environment.’

limitations: The study’s findings are limited to a single major French online retailer, potentially limiting generalizability to other markets or retailer types.
The exploratory nature of the study may not provide definitive causal relationships between events and consumer behavior.

sections.abstract: The COVID-19 pandemic has disrupted retail and accelerated the trend towards electronic commerce. This study explores the reasons for and the implications of this shift, focusing on how consumer behavior evolved during the crisis and the usefulness of a constrained consumer behavior framework in an online environment.
sections.introduction: The study’s objective is to understand how consumers use e-commerce to react to, cope with, and adapt to periods of environmentally imposed constraints (like COVID-19 lockdowns) as evidenced by a shift in purchasing behavior from traditional retail to online channels.
sections.methodology: The study utilizes transaction and search data from a major French online retailer to describe consumer behavior evolution during the COVID-19 crisis. It leverages a multi-perspective react-cope-adapt framework.
sections.results: The results support the usefulness of the multi-perspective react-cope-adapt framework of constrained consumer behavior in an online environment, demonstrating how consumer behavior evolved during the stressful life event of the pandemic.
sections.discussion: The findings highlight how consumers adapted their purchasing patterns in response to the imposed constraints of the lockdown, indicating a shift in consumer behavior towards e-commerce.
sections.related_work: The research builds on consumer behavior literature, emerging COVID-19 research, and the environmentally imposed constraints perspective, positioning itself within the broader field of understanding consumer responses to disruptive events.
sections.conclusion: The study concludes that understanding consumer resilience during periods of environmental constraint, as exemplified by the COVID-19 pandemic, is crucial for understanding and predicting e-commerce behavior.
key_quotes: ‘Our results support the usefulness of the multi-perspective react-cope-adapt framework of constrained consumer behavior in an online environment.’
‘This study explores the reasons for and the implications of this shift.’

properties.title: Understanding Online Consumer Behavior at E-commerce Portals Using Eye-Gaze Tracking
properties.abstract: Abstract E-commerce websites are doing extremely well despite the global slowdown in the traditional brick-and-mortar retail industry. Web page design, search options, and graphical layout may greatly influence the customer base of any e-commerce website. This paper intends to identify the most influential elements of a graphical interface and their effect on consumers’ visual attention. Visual layouts having major influence on customers’ decisions during online shopping were investigated using

key_findings.finding: Graphical interface elements significantly impact user attention, as evidenced by first fixation of eye-gaze and fixation count.
key_findings.evidence: "The results revealed a clear impact of graphical interface on gaining user attention."

key_findings.finding: There are gender-specific visual preferences toward different parts of the graphical user interface.
key_findings.evidence: "Further, the experiment also revealed gender-specific visual preferences toward various parts of graphical user interface."

methodology.approach: The study utilized eye-gaze tracking to investigate the impact of graphical interface elements on consumer visual attention. Data was collected from 76 respondents using heat maps for visualization.
methodology.evidence: "Visual layouts having major influence on customers’ decisions during online shopping were investigated using eye-gaze tracking."

limitations: The study focuses solely on visual attention and does not address other factors influencing consumer behavior.
The specific graphical interfaces investigated are not detailed, limiting the generalizability of the findings.

sections.abstract: E-commerce websites are thriving despite economic headwinds. This paper investigates the impact of graphical interface elements on consumer visual attention, utilizing eye-gaze tracking on 76 respondents to identify areas of maximum user attention and gender-specific visual preferences. The research aims to understand how web page design influences customer decisions during online shopping.
sections.introduction: The paper establishes the context of successful e-commerce despite broader retail challenges. It highlights the importance of web page design, search options, and graphical layout in influencing customer behavior and specifically focuses on understanding how visual attention is directed by these elements within an e-commerce portal.
sections.methodology: The study employed eye-gaze tracking with 76 respondents to analyze user attention on an e-commerce website. Data was collected on first fixation of eye-gaze and fixation count, visualized using heat maps to identify areas of highest user attention.  The research also explored gender-specific visual preferences.
sections.results: The experiment revealed a significant impact of the graphical interface on gaining user attention.  Specifically, the study identified areas of the interface that attracted the most user attention and demonstrated gender-specific visual preferences toward different parts of the graphical user interface.
sections.discussion: The findings suggest that visual layout plays a crucial role in directing consumer attention during online shopping. The identification of gender-specific preferences indicates the need for tailored design approaches to optimize engagement and conversion rates. The results have implications for product, packaging, and promotional design, as well as website graphical interfaces.
sections.related_work: The research builds upon existing work examining the influence of web design on consumer behavior, extending previous studies by utilizing eye-gaze tracking to provide a more granular understanding of visual attention patterns and incorporating gender-specific analysis.
sections.conclusion: The study concludes that graphical interface design significantly impacts consumer attention and engagement in online shopping, with notable gender-specific variations. The findings provide valuable insights for optimizing e-commerce website design to improve user experience and drive sales.
key_quotes: ‘A clear impact of graphical interface on gaining user attention’
‘gender-specific visual preferences toward various parts of graphical user interface’

edges.source: Query Equivalence & Semantic Similarity in E-commerce Search
edges.target: Trend Pattern Identification in Fashion Digital Archives

properties.description: Both papers explore the use of data analysis (query similarity vs. trend identification) to understand consumer behavior, albeit in different domains. The e-commerce paper builds on the concept of finding similar intent, while the fashion paper uses machine learning to identify patterns within a digital archive.

edges.target: Evolution of Consumer Behavior During Disruptive Events

properties.description: The first paper provides a broader framework for understanding how consumers react to crises, while the second paper applies that framework specifically to the COVID-19 pandemic and e-commerce behavior.

The second paper directly investigates how graphical interface elements influence user attention, building upon the broader understanding of visual perception and user experience gained from eye-gaze tracking research.

While one uses statistical methods to analyze language and the other uses machine learning for trend discovery, both contribute to understanding how consumers interpret and respond to marketing information.
